perturbation.original_text: The Licensee will be responsible for immediately reporting to the Licensor any apparent failure of either transmissions or other system failure or delays.
perturbation.changed_text: The Licensee may report to the Licensor any apparent failure of either transmissions or other system failure or delays at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the *requirement* for the licensee to report system failures or delays. This is an omission that weakens consumer protections, as prompt reporting of malfunctions may be essential for legal compliance and consumer safety. Under New York law, businesses have a responsibility to promptly address and resolve technical issues that may impact consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Business Law § 349 (Deceptive acts and practices unlawful)
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the reporting of system failures discretionary rather than mandatory, the company may be shielded from liabilities associated with system malfunctions, which could deceive consumers or allow deceptive acts to continue without prompt correction, potentially violating NY GBL § 349.
perturbation.location: Article II, Section I, paragraph v
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Licensee to report system failures, while the changed version makes reporting discretionary. The Perturbation Explanation states this weakens consumer protections and prompt reporting of malfunctions may be essential for legal compliance and consumer safety. The Law Explanation states this may shield the company from liabilities associated with system malfunctions, potentially violating NY GBL § 349. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because the modification omits a legally mandated consumer protection by removing the requirement to report system failures.

perturbation.original_text: The Licensee agrees to notify the Licensor in writing (including by email) or telephone promptly following the discovery of any Error.
perturbation.changed_text: The Licensee may notify the Licensor of any Error.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prompt notification of errors and allowing it to be discretionary weakens consumer protections. Prompt error reporting is crucial for identifying and fixing issues, protecting consumers from potential harm. Making error reporting optional, potentially shields the licensor from liabilities related to undiscovered or unreported errors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Business Law § 349 (Deceptive acts and practices unlawful)
perturbation.law_explanation: By making error reporting optional, there is increased potential for errors to persist, misleading customers and potentially violating NY GBL § 349.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates prompt notification of errors, while the changed version makes it discretionary. This omission removes a legally important consumer protection, creating a loophole where errors may go unreported, potentially harming consumers. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted, rendering the contract non-compliant. The Law Explanation supports this by highlighting the increased potential for errors to persist, potentially violating NY GBL § 349, due to the discretionary nature of error reporting in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee will not delete or in any manner alter the copyright, or other proprietary rights, notices of Licensor appearing on or in the Licensed Technology as delivered to Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee will use reasonable efforts not to delete or in any manner alter the copyright, or other proprietary rights, notices of Licensor appearing on or in the Licensed Technology as delivered to Licensee.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will not delete' to 'will use reasonable efforts not to delete', the contract provides a loophole for the licensee to alter or delete copyright notices. This introduces legal risk as it weakens the protection of intellectual property rights, which are federally protected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 1202 - Integrity of copyright management information
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the protection of copyright management information, as the licensee is only required to use 'reasonable efforts' rather than strictly prohibiting alteration or deletion of copyright notices, thus creating a legal risk in contravention of 17 U.S. Code § 1202. The Digital Millennium Copyright Act makes it illegal to remove or alter copyright management information with the intent to infringe copyright.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 12 / § 1202 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 1202 : Integrity of copyright management information Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 12-COPYRIGHT PROTECTION AND MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments §1202. Integrity of copyright management information (a) False Copyright Management Information .-No person shall knowingly and with the intent to induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal infringement- (1) provide copyright management information that is false, or (2) distribute or import for distribution copyright management information that is false. (b) Removal or Alteration of Copyright Management Information .-No pers
perturbation.justification: The original contract language, 'will not delete,' creates a strict obligation. The changed text, 'will use reasonable efforts not to delete,' weakens this obligation by introducing a subjective standard of 'reasonable efforts.' According to 17 U.S. Code § 1202, 'No person shall knowingly and with the intent to induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal infringement... remove or Alteration of Copyright Management Information .' The modified text creates a loophole, potentially allowing the licensee to delete or alter copyright notices if they deem it 'unreasonable' to prevent it. This directly contradicts the strict prohibition in the law against altering copyright management information, as the 'reasonable efforts' clause provides a basis to justify actions that would otherwise be a violation.
